meta.title: BDD & Acceptance Testing
meta.description: Cucumber, Gherkin, acceptance test-driven development, and behavior-driven testing patterns

items.text: Are scenarios written in business language (not technical implementation)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Gherkin scenarios should describe behavior from the user's perspective: 'When I log in' not 'When I POST to /api/auth/login'.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Business-language scenarios serve as living documentation. Technical scenarios are unreadable to stakeholders and become brittle.
baseContent.howToVerify: Read each scenario from a product owner's perspective. If they can't understand it without code knowledge, it's too technical.
baseContent.exampleComment: 'When the user sends a POST to /api/orders' is implementation detail. Write 'When the user places an order' instead.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Write scenarios in business language. They should be readable by non-developers.

items.text: Does each scenario test exactly one behavior?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Each scenario should verify one specific behavior with one clear outcome. Multiple behaviors belong in separate scenarios.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Multi-behavior scenarios are hard to debug (which behavior failed?), hard to name, and hard to maintain.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if the scenario has multiple Then assertions testing unrelated things. Each should be a separate scenario.
baseContent.exampleComment: This scenario tests login, profile update, AND logout. Split into 3 scenarios, each testing one behavior.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: One scenario, one behavior. If you need 'And' in Then more than twice, consider splitting.

items.text: Is the Given/When/Then structure used correctly?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Given = preconditions/state. When = action being tested. Then = expected outcome. Don't mix these roles.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Wrong structure makes scenarios confusing. A When that sets up state or a Given that asserts results breaks the pattern's clarity.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that Givens only set up state, Whens only perform actions, and Thens only assert outcomes.
baseContent.exampleComment: 'When the user has an account' describes state, not an action. This should be a Given. When should describe what the user does.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Given = arrange, When = act, Then = assert. Follow this strictly.

items.text: Are Scenario Outlines used for parameterized tests?

baseContent.whatItMeans: When multiple scenarios differ only in data values, use Scenario Outline with Examples tables to reduce duplication.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Copy-pasted scenarios with different values are hard to maintain. Adding a new case requires writing the entire scenario again.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for scenarios that are identical except for specific values. These should use Scenario Outline with Examples.
baseContent.exampleComment: These 5 scenarios are identical except for the role name. Use a Scenario Outline with an Examples table of roles.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use Scenario Outline for data-driven scenarios. Keep Examples tables focused and readable.

items.text: Is Background used appropriately for shared preconditions?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Background runs before every scenario in a feature. Use it for common setup steps. Don't put too many steps in Background.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Repeated Given steps across all scenarios should be in Background. But a complex Background makes scenarios hard to read in isolation.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for Given steps repeated in every scenario — these should be in Background. Verify Background has 2-3 steps maximum.
baseContent.exampleComment: Every scenario starts with 'Given a logged-in admin user'. Move this to the Background section.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use Background for steps common to all scenarios. Keep it short (2-3 steps max).

items.text: Are step definitions reusable and not duplicated?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Step definitions should be written once and reused across scenarios. Use regex/cucumber expression parameters for flexibility.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Duplicate step definitions cause ambiguous step errors and maintenance burden. A change in behavior requires updating multiple steps.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for similar step definitions with slightly different wording. These should be consolidated with parameters.
baseContent.exampleComment: 'When I click the login button' and 'When I click the submit button' should be one step: 'When I click the {string} button'.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Write parameterized step definitions. Reuse steps across scenarios.

items.text: Are step definitions thin (delegating to page objects or helper methods)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Step definitions should be a thin layer that maps Gherkin to code. Complex logic belongs in page objects, helpers, or domain code.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Fat step definitions are hard to maintain and can't be reused. Logic in steps is duplicated instead of shared.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check step definitions for more than 5 lines of code. Complex Selenium/API interactions should be in helper classes.
baseContent.exampleComment: This step definition has 30 lines of Selenium interactions. Extract into a LoginPage class with a login() method.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Keep step definitions thin. Delegate to page objects, API helpers, or domain services.

items.text: Is scenario state managed properly (not leaked between scenarios)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Each scenario should start with a clean state. Use hooks (Before/After) for setup and teardown. Don't rely on scenario execution order.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Shared state between scenarios causes flaky tests. A scenario that passes alone fails when another scenario runs first.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for static variables or global state in step definitions. Verify Before hooks reset state. Check for order-dependent scenarios.
baseContent.exampleComment: This step definition stores the user in a static field. When scenarios run in parallel, they share/overwrite each other's user.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Isolate scenario state completely. Use Before/After hooks for setup and cleanup.

items.text: Do Then steps have clear, specific assertions?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Then steps should assert specific, verifiable outcomes. 'Then the order is created' should check a specific order property, not just non-null.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Vague assertions pass even when the behavior is wrong. 'Then something happened' tests nothing.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check Then step implementations. Verify they assert on specific values, not just existence or non-null.
baseContent.exampleComment: 'Then the order is created' only checks order != null. Verify the order has correct items, total, and status.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Assert on specific outcomes. A Then step that can't fail is useless.

items.text: Are features organized by business capability (not by technical layer)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Organize feature files by business domain (ordering, authentication, reporting), not by technical layer (api, ui, database).
baseContent.whyItMatters: Business-organized features map to team ownership and make it easy to find relevant tests. Technical organization scatters related tests.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check the feature file directory structure. Look for business-domain folders rather than technical-layer folders.
baseContent.exampleComment: Features are organized as /api/orders.feature and /ui/orders.feature. Merge into /ordering/place-order.feature.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Organize by business capability. One feature per business workflow.

items.text: Are tags used to categorize and control test execution?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use tags (@smoke, @regression, @wip, @slow) to categorize scenarios. Run subsets by tag in CI for faster feedback.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without tags, you must run all tests or none. Tags enable smoke tests on PR, full regression nightly, and skipping WIP tests.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for tag usage on features and scenarios. Verify CI uses tags to run appropriate subsets.
baseContent.exampleComment: This smoke test suite takes 45 minutes because it includes all tests. Tag critical paths with @smoke and run only those on PR.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Tag scenarios for selective execution. Use @smoke for fast CI feedback, full suite for nightly.

items.text: Is test data set up through the application (not direct DB manipulation)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Create test data through the application's API or UI, not by inserting directly into the database. This tests the full stack.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Direct DB inserts bypass validation, triggers, and business logic. Tests pass but the same operation fails through the UI.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check Given step implementations. Verify they use API calls or UI actions, not raw SQL or ORM inserts.
baseContent.exampleComment: 'Given a user exists' inserts directly into the database, bypassing registration validation. Use the registration API instead.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Set up test data through the application. Direct DB manipulation hides integration issues.

items.text: Can scenarios run independently in any order?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Each scenario must set up its own preconditions and clean up after itself. No scenario should depend on another running first.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Order-dependent tests fail randomly in parallel execution and are impossible to debug. They also can't be run individually.
baseContent.howToVerify: Run a single scenario in isolation. If it fails without other scenarios running first, it has hidden dependencies.
baseContent.exampleComment: This scenario assumes the user from the previous scenario still exists. Add a Given step to create its own user.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Every scenario must be self-contained. Run in any order, run in parallel, run alone.

items.text: Are UI implementation details kept out of scenarios?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Don't reference CSS selectors, button IDs, or page structure in Gherkin. 'When I click #btn-submit' is too technical.
baseContent.whyItMatters: UI details in scenarios break tests when the UI changes. Scenarios should survive redesigns that don't change behavior.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for CSS selectors, XPath, HTML element names, or page-specific terminology in Gherkin steps.
baseContent.exampleComment: 'When I click the div.submit-btn' should be 'When I submit the form'. Keep UI details in step definitions.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Describe what users do, not how the UI is structured. 'Submit the form' not 'click the button'.

items.text: Are scenarios free of incidental details (irrelevant data)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Only include data relevant to the behavior being tested. Don't specify user's email if the test is about order placement.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Incidental details distract from the behavior being tested. Readers can't tell which details matter and which are noise.
baseContent.howToVerify: For each piece of data in a scenario, ask: 'Does this affect the outcome?' If not, remove it or use a default.
baseContent.exampleComment: This scenario specifies the user's full address, phone, and birthday for a login test. Only username and password are relevant.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Include only data relevant to the behavior. Hide everything else behind defaults in step definitions.

items.text: Are scenarios declarative (not imperative step-by-step UI instructions)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Write 'When I log in as admin' (declarative) not 'When I go to /login, And I type admin, And I type password, And I click Login' (imperative).
baseContent.whyItMatters: Imperative scenarios are verbose, fragile, and hard to read. They break when UI steps change even if behavior doesn't.
baseContent.howToVerify: Count the steps in each scenario. More than 7-8 steps usually indicates imperative style. Look for click/type/navigate actions.
baseContent.exampleComment: This 15-step scenario walks through every form field. Collapse into 3 declarative steps: Given/When/Then.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Be declarative. 'When I place an order' not 'When I click add to cart, And I click checkout, And I fill address...'.

items.text: Is BDD used only where it adds value (not for every test)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: BDD is best for acceptance tests that verify business behavior. Unit tests and integration tests don't need Gherkin.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Writing unit tests in Gherkin adds overhead without benefit. The Gherkin layer slows development and obscures simple test logic.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for Cucumber scenarios testing implementation details (sorting algorithms, helper functions). These should be unit tests.
baseContent.exampleComment: This Cucumber scenario tests a utility function that formats dates. Write a simple unit test instead — it's faster and clearer.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use BDD for acceptance tests (business behavior). Use unit/integration tests for technical behavior.
